Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: First up, our former South Carolina Republican Senator Tim Scott and Congressman

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Trey Gowdy.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: They are out with a brand new book together on divisiveness in D.C., they say they got

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: the idea for the book after the 2015 church shooting in Charleston, South Carolina.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: The book is called Unified, How Our Unlikely Friendship Gives us Hope for a Divided Country.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Joining me now, Senator Scott and Congressman Gowdy.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: So John, we're going to get to the book in just a moment.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: First up, a shooting brought the two of you together, a horrific tragedy in your home state.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Are guns tearing us apart?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I think the gun issue you could argue is, and I think there's a pretty reasonable way

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: to approach it is we just don't do it that way.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Our approach to debating this is you don't support the second amendment and you support

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: mass killings.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And certainly there's something in the middle, I was a gun prosecutor for almost two decades.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So what I always say is, how are we doing forcing the current panoply of gun laws that

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: we have?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And the reality is, for eight years, even under President Obama, gun prosecutions went

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: down.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So if you're advocating for a new group of laws to enforce, one of the first questions you're

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: going to get is, how are you doing with the current crop of laws?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And if the answer is not good, you have a hard time advocating for new ones.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Sorry, I had to lean into your shop there.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: They had to grab something from my chair.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: It's a live show.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: It happens.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: But there are a lot of laws on the books, and there are a vast majority of gun owners

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: in this country are trained, they are self-aware, and they're very cognizant of those laws and

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: of gun safety.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I think it's very disingenuous when we have tragedies like this to immediately spew talking

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: points as we've seen from some of these CEOs is, Deirdre Bolton point out, how do we elevate

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: the conversation?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Well, we have to get to the main.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: There is a common denominator that we should pay more attention to, and that's the mental

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: health of these shooters.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: The reality of it is that when you take a look at the one fight that seems to be pervasive

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: after mass shooting, after mass shooting, there is a mental instability that is a part

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and parcel to the actual conversation that we're not having.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: We're running to the weapon of choice.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: When, in fact, brothers, the bombs in Austin, shootings in Charleston, shootings in California,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: the one thing that we basically see consistently are suicides that follow a lot of these shootings.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I'd say that we're missing a key element and we should spend more time on that common

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: denominator.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And we do, but we also have to be careful not to paint mental illness with a broad brush

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and not personify objects because I look at the person who tried to mow down and kill as

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: many people as he could on Halloween in a car on a bike path here in New York City.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I look at the Austin bomber, I look at the parkland shooter, and we don't know much about

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: the shooting today, but the element is these are people who are able to dehumanize other

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: people and they are so disconnected from human nature and the common bond that we have and

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: that, as a parent that worries me.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I think you said earlier Kennedy that you can't legislate against hate and it's very hard

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: to see into the soul and see how dark that soul really is without being able to do those

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: two things.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: You have to look for things that unify our country and find a rallying point.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I think the common denominator is a very important part of the equation.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I also think the conversation that we need to have as a country continues to unfold.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Listen, less than a month ago we passed an expansion of the background check system in

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: the last legislative vehicle that passed through Congress.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: The reality of it is we continue to address the issue, but we're not addressing the fine

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: points within the issue because it's not popular yet.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And also being disingenuous about it, saying things like we don't have any background checks.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: That's simply not true.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: What is true is I think people want us to come together.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I think people want commonality.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I think when people see the president sitting down with Chuck Schumer and Nancy Pelosi they're

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: curious about it, but they want something positive and instructive to come from that.
